qilin.description: Qilin ransomware was first observed in July of 2022. Qilin Ransomware is written in Golang and supports multiple encryption modes; all of which are controlled by the operator. Qilin actors practice double extortion – demanding payment for a decryptor, as well as for the non-release of stolen data.

techniques.technique_details: Compromised credentials used to authenticate via VPN and RDP. Qilin notably steals credentials from Chrome browsers on compromised endpoints before encryption.
Exploitation of vulnerabilities in VPN appliances and remote management tools to gain initial access.
Qilin affiliates spear-phished MSP administrators via ScreenConnect to gain access to downstream customers. Phishing campaigns using credential-harvesting pages targeting VPN and SSO portals.
PowerShell scripts used for payload execution, lateral movement, and post-exploitation tasks.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
techniques.technique_details: Shell scripts used to execute the Linux/ESXi variant of the Qilin ransomware payload.
Scheduled tasks created to maintain persistence and execute post-exploitation scripts.
Qilin.B variant (October 2024) introduced enhanced obfuscation and anti-analysis techniques compared to earlier versions.

techniques.technique_name: Access Token Manipulation: Parent PID Spoofing

techniques.technique_details: Security tools and EDR solutions disabled via batch scripts and Group Policy modifications.

techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

techniques.technique_details: LSASS memory dumped to harvest credentials for lateral movement and privilege escalation.

Qilin notably deployed a custom script to steal credentials stored in Google Chrome browsers on all domain-joined machines via Group Policy — a distinctive TTP first observed in the Synnovis NHS attack (June 2024).
Network scanning used to enumerate hosts and services within the victim environment.
System information collected to tailor the attack and identify high-value targets.
RDP used for lateral movement across victim networks.
SMB used to propagate payloads and move laterally within the network.

techniques.technique_name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility
techniques.technique_details: Data compressed and archived prior to exfiltration.

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Other Network Medium: Exfiltration Over Bluetooth
Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
techniques.technique_details: Data exfiltrated to cloud storage and actor-controlled servers for double extortion via the Qilin leak site.
C2 communication over HTTPS; Tor used for victim portals.

Qilin (formerly Agenda) uses ChaCha20 for file encryption with RSA-4096 for key protection. Originally written in Go, later rewritten in Rust (Qilin.B). Targets Windows, Linux, and VMware ESXi. Notable attack: Synnovis NHS pathology services (June 2024) causing 10,000+ NHS appointment cancellations. Active RaaS with high affiliate revenue share.
Shadow copies deleted; ESXi snapshots removed to prevent VM recovery.

techniques.technique_name: Gather Victim Network Information: Network Topology

DefenseEvasion: Updater for Carbon Black’s Cloud Sensor AV (upd.exe)

akira.description: The akira ransomware group is a highly sophisticated cybercriminal syndicate known for target-rich double extortion campaigns. Sourcing initial access widely, often through infostealer credential sales on underground forums.

clop.description: The clop ransomware group is a highly sophisticated cybercriminal syndicate known for target-rich double extortion campaigns. Sourcing initial access widely, often through infostealer credential sales on underground forums.

incransom.description: INC Ransom is a prolific ransomware-as-a-service operation active since July 2023 that systematically targets healthcare, government, education, and manufacturing sectors in North America and Europe, having posted over 200 victims in 2025 alone with no sector off-limits.

play.description: The play ransomware group is a highly sophisticated cybercriminal syndicate known for target-rich double extortion campaigns. Sourcing initial access widely, often through infostealer credential sales on underground forums.

safepay.description: SafePay emerged in September 2024 as a rapidly growing ransomware operation that explicitly disavows the RaaS model and manages all operations internally, claiming over 300 victims worldwide by mid-2025 with a high-profile early attack against UK telematics firm Microlise stealing 1.2 TB of data.

techniques.technique_details: The threat actor accessed the endpoint via Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) using valid credentials.
Employed WMI commands to execute processes on remote systems.
Utilized PowerShell scripts, such as ShareFinder.ps1, to execute commands on the compromised system.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Windows Command Shell
Command and Scripting Interpreter: Visual Basic

techniques.technique_details: Maintained access through the use of compromised valid accounts.

Conducted domain trust discovery using commands like 'net group domain admins /domain' and 'nltest.exe'.

techniques.technique_details: Moved laterally within the network using Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) and Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI).
Archived files using WinRAR with specific command-line options to prepare data for exfiltration.

Deleted volume shadow copies to inhibit system recovery.

Gather Victim Org Information: Identify Roles

thegentlemen.description: The Gentlemen is a RaaS group that emerged in July–August 2025, rapidly claiming over 320 victims across 17+ countries by offering affiliates a 90% revenue share, deploying a Go-based locker against Windows, Linux, NAS, and BSD systems; a compromised C2 server in 2026 revealed more than 1,570 linked victims.

techniques.technique_details: Use of valid accounts for initial access.
Use of valid domain accounts for initial access.
openconnect used to authenticate to FortiGate SSL VPN (--protocol=fortinet). Actors connect post-credential-acquisition to enumerate internal network routes.
Exploitation of public-facing applications for initial access.

Spear-phishing via compromised corporate OWA mailboxes. ZIP payloads containing LNK files disguised as PDF/DOC/XLS or malicious Office documents sent from legitimate company email accounts to bypass spam filters.
NetExec (nxc) with -X/-x flags for remote PowerShell/CMD execution over WMI/SMB. TrustedSec Titanis used for WMI-based remote execution via NTLM hashes.
Use of command and scripting interpreters for execution.
Use of PowerShell for execution.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Windows Command Shell
techniques.technique_details: Use of Windows Command Shell for execution.
Ransomware locker and EDR killers deployed domain-wide via GPO (deploy_gpo.ps1). GPO configured to run binaries at startup on all domain-joined machines.
Creation of accounts for persistence.
Velociraptor deployed as a SYSTEM-level Windows service via MSI (Server.Utils.CreateMSI artifact) for persistent beacon access.
Boot or logon autostart execution for persistence.
Exploitation of vulnerabilities for privilege escalation.
PetitPotam coercion via NXC coerce_plus module to trigger NTLM authentication from domain hosts toward attacker-controlled relay listener.
Responder (SMB/HTTP disabled) combined with ntlmrelayx targeting LDAP for machine account creation. RelayKing used to scan SMB/LDAP/MSSQL/HTTP/RPC/WinRM relay opportunities.
Obfuscation of files or information to evade detection.
Automated post-compromise script clears all Windows Event Logs via wevtutil, removes RDP MRU registry keys, deletes .rdp files, empties Recycle Bin, and clears RDP/SMB/WinRM logs.
ProxyChains (Windows/Linux), SSH dynamic port forwarding (-NfD 1080), ProxyJump multi-hop pivoting. Self-hosted double-VPN with WireGuard/OpenVPN and Amnezia VPN for operator anonymity.
Modifying the registry for defense evasion.

techniques.technique_name: Domain Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification
techniques.technique_details: Modification of Group Policy for defense evasion.
Impairing defenses to avoid detection.

techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
techniques.technique_details: BYOVD drivers ($3500-$5000 darknet), EDRStartupHinder, GFreeze/GLinker against CrowdStrike, IFEO registry redirecting EDR processes to calc.exe, WMI GlobalLogger/WPR symlink attacks to overwrite EDR binaries on boot.

XenAllPasswordPro deployed via SMB for mass browser credential harvesting across all hosts. KslDump/KslKatz for LSASS dumping. Velociraptor for full memory dumps without AV detection. Pass-the-Hash via xfreerdp.

Custom FortiGate panel bruter on dedicated hardware (dual Xeon, 120GB RAM) targeting non-standard ports. Credential spraying with known default passwords. Hydra for email spraying. Hash cracking via crackmd5.ru and chamd5.org.
FortiGate configuration files exfiltrated containing plaintext LDAP credentials, local VPN user passwords, and IPSec pre-shared keys. MANSPIDER used to search SMB shares for credential files.
DumpBrowserSecrets used for browser credential and session cookie theft. Cookies imported via Cookie-Editor extension to hijack authenticated M365/CRM/email sessions.
gogo internal port scanner across /24 subnets (ports 22,53,80,88,389,443,445,636,3389,1433,5985) with 100 threads and ICMP ping. NetExec for SMB/WinRM/LDAP host enumeration.
Discovery of network services.
BloodHound/CertiHound for AD and AD CS (ESC1-ESC17) enumeration. ADFind and ldapdomaindump for group/permission mapping. PrivHound for LPE vector identification within AD.
Discovery of accounts within the environment.
Discovery of domain accounts within the environment.
Discovery of domain trust relationships.
AWS S3 buckets and EKS clusters enumerated post-access. Censys, Shodan, ZoomInfo, and c99.nl API used for external recon of VPN endpoints and subdomains.
Use of remote services for lateral movement.
Use of RDP for lateral movement.
Use of SMB/Windows Admin Shares for lateral movement.
Use of SSH for lateral movement.
Browser session cookies stolen via DumpBrowserSecrets and replayed via Cookie-Editor to hijack authenticated web application sessions (email, M365, CRM portals).
XenAllPasswordPro HTML credential reports collected per host. SQL database full dumps via phpMyAdmin exported to SFTP. File system enumeration targeting CRM code, backups, and financial data.
Collection of data from network shared drives.
Staging of collected data prior to exfiltration.
Local staging of collected data prior to exfiltration.
Corporate OWA mailboxes accessed via purchased stealer logs (snusbase.com) to harvest internal communications, credentials, and sensitive documents.
Exfiltration of data over encrypted channels.

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Symmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
techniques.technique_details: Exfiltration over symmetric encrypted non-C2 channels.
rclone to actor-controlled SFTP servers with high-parallelism (--transfers 16 --multi-thread-streams 8 --buffer-size 256M). Observed volumes: 13GB NAS, 3+TB Docker/Nexus, 100+GB Confluence/JIRA.
Use of application layer protocols for C2 communication.
Use of web protocols for C2 communication.
Use of remote access software for C2.
SSH dynamic SOCKS proxies (ssh -NfD 1080) for network pivoting. ProxyChains (Windows/Linux) tunneling attack tools through SOCKS5 proxies acquired from spam/malware affiliates.
Cloudflare Tunnels blend C2 with legitimate HTTPS traffic. Chisel-ng (Rust, SSH-over-WebSocket-over-TLS) for reverse tunnels. Velociraptor C2 over TLS with signed MSI beacons.
Encryption of data for extortion.
Stopping services to maximize impact.

Veeam backup jobs stopped and tape media long-erased (Erase-VBRTapeMedium -Long). Docker and QEMU VMs killed pre-encryption. vCenter used to reset ESXi root passwords. Database services (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis, MSSQL) stopped before encryption.
Data deleted from CRM web panels and databases. chmod -R 777 applied to NAS filesystems to facilitate locker access and destroy access controls.

dragonforce.description: DragonForce is a major ransomware-as-a-service operation first observed in August 2023 that launched a formal affiliate program offering 80% revenue share, then rebranded as a "ransomware cartel" in 2025, gaining notoriety for high-profile attacks on UK retailers Marks & Spencer, Co-op, and Harrods.

techniques.technique_details: Executes a malicious file on the victim's system.
The ransomware self-deletes after execution.

techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
techniques.technique_details: Disables Windows Defender (if it is running).
The ransomware enumerates directories to encrypt files.
The ransomware uses data encryption to extort the victim.

lynx.description: Lynx is a ransomware-as-a-service operation that emerged in mid-2024 as a rebrand of INC Ransomware (whose source code was sold for $300,000 on the RAMP forum), claiming ~300 victims across manufacturing, business services, technology, and transportation with an 80/20 profit split for affiliates.

techniques.technique_details: Compromised credentials purchased from initial access brokers used to authenticate via RDP and VPN.
Lynx uses phishing emails with malicious attachments to deliver initial access stagers.
PowerShell used for payload execution and post-exploitation activity.
Lynx ransomware binary renamed to blend into normal file system activity; masquerades as legitimate utilities.
Security tools disabled before ransomware deployment.
Credential dumping from LSASS used to facilitate lateral movement.
Registry queried to identify installed security software and system configuration before encryption.
Lynx queries CPU information and system details to assess the target environment and check for sandbox/analysis conditions.
RDP used for lateral movement with harvested credentials.

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
techniques.technique_details: Data exfiltrated for double extortion via the Lynx leak site prior to encryption.
C2 communication over HTTPS; Tor used for victim negotiation portals.

Lynx uses AES-128 CTR mode for file encryption with RSA-2048 for key protection. Code similarities indicate it may be a rebrand or fork of INC Ransomware. Emerged mid-2024; highly aggressive targeting of SMBs and mid-market organizations across multiple sectors. Appends .lynx extension.
Shadow copies deleted and recovery options disabled to prevent victim restoration.

nightspire.description: NightSpire is a ransomware group that first emerged in March 2025 and rapidly claimed over 250 victims across retail, manufacturing, healthcare, finance, and education sectors in the US, France, India, Taiwan, and Japan, using aggressive double-extortion with ransom deadlines as short as two days.

techniques.technique_details: Compromised RDP credentials used for initial access.
Brute-forcing remote login credentials (RDP) and MFA fatigue attacks.
Exploitation of CVE-2024-55591 — FortiOS/FortiProxy authentication bypass; unauthenticated attackers gain super-admin privileges via crafted POST requests to /api/v2/cmdb/.
Malicious attachments and drive-by downloads.
PowerShell scripts, batch files, PsExec, WMI; conhost.exe command execution window.
Abuse of legitimate tools (WinSCP, MEGACmd, 7-Zip, PsExec) across the attack chain.
Persistence via Windows Task Scheduler; service creation and modification.
Administrative account creation post-exploitation on FortiGate devices.
Reboot persistence mechanisms.

techniques.technique_name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
techniques.technique_details: Persistence via HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run and RunOnce.
FortiOS super-admin access via CVE-2024-55591 exploitation.
Obfuscation techniques to evade analysis.
Renamed processes and use of legitimate tools (WinSCP, MEGACmd, 7-Zip, PsExec) blending into normal operations.
Removal of forensic indicators from compromised systems.
Execution via legitimate system binaries (LOLBins) to evade detection.
Credential dumping via Mimikatz.
LSASS memory extraction for credential harvesting.
Harvesting of stored credentials within the environment.
Network scanning to map internal infrastructure using Advanced IP Scanner.
Process enumeration on compromised systems.
Collection of system details from compromised hosts.
File indexing and enumeration using Everything.exe.
RDP-based lateral movement across compromised network.
Lateral movement via PsExec over SMB.
WMI-based execution and lateral movement.
Automated sensitive data gathering from compromised systems.
Compression of collected data using 7-Zip (7z2408-x64.exe) prior to exfiltration.

techniques.technique_name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility
techniques.technique_details: 7-Zip archiving of stolen data prior to exfiltration.
Data exfiltration over C2 channel.
Data exfiltration via WinSCP (v6.3.7) and Rclone over encrypted channels.

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
techniques.technique_details: MEGACmd used to upload stolen data to MEGA cloud storage. Documented exfiltration of 1.5TB from a single healthcare victim.
Standard web protocols and Tor-based communication. Multi-channel comms: ProtonMail, OnionMail, Gmail, Telegram, qTox.
Asymmetric encrypted non-C2 protocols used to evade IDS.
Hybrid AES-256 (file content) + RSA-2048 (key protection) encryption; appends .nspire extension; processes files in 1MB block chunks. Double extortion model — data theft + encryption.
Custom Go-based ransomware development with modular architecture.

sinobi.description: Sinobi is a private vetted-affiliate RaaS group that emerged in mid-2025, believed to be a rebrand of the Lynx/INC ransomware lineage, claiming 176 victims by end of 2025 through double-extortion attacks primarily against mid-market US organizations via compromised SonicWall VPN credentials.

techniques.technique_details: Use of valid credentials from MSPs (Managed Service Providers).
Exploitation of vulnerabilities in VPNs (SonicWall).
Heavy use of PowerShell for script and in-memory command execution.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Windows Command Shell
Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service
techniques.technique_details: Creation or modification of Windows services to ensure malware restart.
Removal of event logs.

techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
techniques.technique_details: Active disabling of EDR solutions (such as VMware Carbon Black).
Use of RDP to navigate between servers after privilege escalation.

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
techniques.technique_details: Use of Rclone tool to send data to public cloud providers before encryption.
Data encryption via AES-128-CTR and Curve-25519, adding the .SINOBI extension.
Deletion of Shadow Copies via vssadmin.exe.

lockbit5.description: LockBit 5.0 ("ChuongDong") emerged in September 2025 as the group's resurgence following the February 2024 law enforcement takedown, introducing cross-platform payloads targeting Windows, Linux, and VMware ESXi with enhanced evasion capabilities and continuing the RaaS affiliate model of its predecessors.

ransomhub.description:
The group emerged in mid-February 2024 and has already listed several organizations as alleged victims of their attacks, resulting from extortion through encryption and data leaks.

The announcement of the sale of the new Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) by RansomHub was published on one of the Russian-origin forums used by cybercrime to advertise malicious services, known as RAMP4U (or RAMP). A user with the nickname and persona of 'koley' announced the affiliate program on February 2, 2024.

In the new RaaS announcement, it was mentioned that the money laundering operation of the paid ransoms is the responsibility of the affiliate. This means that all communication and sending of the decryptor to the victim are done through chat. The split of this RaaS would be 90% of the value for the affiliate and 10% for the developer, who in this case would be the persona of Koley.

Furthermore, according to the publication, the ransomware payload is written in Golang language, uses the asymmetric algorithm based on x25519, and encryption algorithms AES256, ChaCha20, and xChaCha20, standing out for its speed. The encryption is obfuscated using AST.

The payload would support network propagation and encryption of data both in secure and local mode. According to Koley, the ransomware is designed to operate on platforms such as Windows, Linux, and ESXi, as well as other architectures such as ARM and MIPS.

As pointed out by the panel and already highlighted by the intelligence team, Koley stated that the panel uses a .onion domain, allowing the affiliate to organize and manage targets and chat rooms, view access logs, automatically respond when offline, and create private blog pages.
Source: https://github.com/crocodyli/ThreatActors-TTPs

techniques.technique_details: The ransomware deletes shadow copies using the WMIC.exe utility.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Windows Command Shell
techniques.technique_details: The ransomware utilizes cmd.exe to execute various Windows utilities to implement various other techniques.

techniques.technique_name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Winlogon Helper DLL
Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass UAC
Obfuscated Files or Information: Embedded Payloads
Obfuscated Files or Information: Encrypted/Encoded File
Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
techniques.technique_details: The ransomware clears the victim machine's application, system, and security event logs using the wevtutil.exe utility.

techniques.technique_name: File and Directory Permissions Modification: Windows Permissions
Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Group Policy Modification
Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
techniques.technique_details: Threat actors use files such as: STONESTOP and POORTRY to load drivers for the purpose of disabling and deleting AV files.

techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
Credentials from Password Stores: Password Managers

techniques.technique_details: Affiliates were identified using: psexec.exe, PsExec.exe, and smbexec.exe for lateral movement.

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Asymmetric Encrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage

techniques.technique_details: Files are encrypted using file replacement method.

The Windows IIS service stop command is executed using iisreset.exe. Allows for encryption of web applications hosted on IIS servers as files linked to these applications are typically locked while IIS is running.
The ransomware deletes system shadow copies to inhibit system recovery.

devman.description: Former RansomHub and INC Ransom affiliate.

techniques.technique_details: Use of valid credentials (malharbi) to access systems.
Use of the MS17-010 (EternalBlue) exploit.
Execution of PowerShell commands to extract files.
Command execution using MS17-010 via Metasploit.
Maintaining access with created administrator account.
Escalation to SYSTEM privileges.
Use of an innocuous name for the ransomware payload (iamdidy.e).
No security tools detected on target systems.
Implied by acquisition and use of admin credentials.
Network mapping using CrackMapExec.
Scanning IP ranges and SMB services.
Using tasklist and whoami to collect system info.
Lateral movement through SMB confirmed by CME.
File extraction from local system using PowerShell.
Downloading files like notepad.exe via smbclient.
Encryption of files with .devman extension (changed at operator's request).
Halting operations and rendering systems unavailable.
Disabling backups and system recovery.
Ransom notes (ransom.txt) deployed across infected systems.

babuk2.description: Babuk Locker 2.0, also known as Bjorka or SkyWave, after failing to make any profit from selling public databases on forums, decided to impersonate Babuk Ransomware group. He launched a blog where he claimed multiple public breaches from BreachForums as ransomware attacks

coinbasecartel.description: CoinbaseCartel specializes in data acquisition through system access and strategic partnerships. It focus exclusively on data exfiltration—our operations never involve system encryption or operational disruption.

techniques.technique_details: Massive focus on credential theft from SaaS platforms (Salesforce, Microsoft 365).
Although less common, there are records of attempts via service providers (corrupted insiders/third parties).
Abuse of VPNs and RDP using credentials harvested by infostealers or purchased from IABs (Initial Access Brokers).
Use of voice social engineering to induce employees to authorize malicious OAuth applications.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
techniques.technique_details: The shinysp1d3r loader is executed via shell scripts on ESXi systems.
Use of custom scripts that mimic legitimate tools (e.g., Salesforce Data Loader) for rapid exfiltration.
Deceiving users into executing fake OAuth connectors.
Adding new secrets or keys to existing OAuth applications to maintain access even after password changes.
Creation of "ghost" administrator users on VMware hosts.

techniques.technique_name: Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location
techniques.technique_details: Renaming malicious binaries to names of critical VMware processes or backup tools.

techniques.technique_name: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
techniques.technique_details: Systematic cleaning of syslogs and audit logs in ESXi environments.

techniques.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
techniques.technique_details: Disabling virtual machine snapshots before encryption (when they opt for it).
Enumeration of Active Directory objects from non-privileged user accounts.
Scanning vCenter datastores to identify critical VMs and databases.
Exploration of AWS/Azure consoles to identify EBS volumes and S3 instances.

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
techniques.technique_details: Use of tools like Rclone to send data to providers such as Mega or Dropbox.
Secondary technique, focused on .vmdk files via shinysp1d3r loader.
Direct extortion based on the threat of leaking data on Onion forums (DLS).

everest.description: Everest ransom group collects and analyzes information about their victims. They specialize in customer privacy data, financial information, databases, credit card information, and more. The Everest ransom group leaks the victim's data to the darknet and they announced that any victim that will not contact them will suffer from a data leak and they will not delete hist files for future usage.

techniques.technique_details: Binary protected with ConfuserEx: rename obfuscation, constants encryption, integer confusion, and modified module .cctor.

techniques.technique_name: Obfuscated Files or Information: Dynamic API Resolution
techniques.technique_details: All non-corlib native API calls resolved at runtime via LoadLibrary / GetProcAddress rather than static imports.
Implicit current-user impersonation used for net use \\<unc> connections to discovered network shares without explicit credential passing.
Multiple HKCU/HKLM registry writes: LongPathsEnabled, LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy, EnableLinkedConnections (share access), WallPaper (ransom note), AppName\PublicKey (encryption key storage).
Runtime decryption of UserStrings via <Module>.m using GZip decompression followed by Base64 decoding.
Global <GUID> mutex enforces single-instance execution; CIS culture/LCID geo-fence prevents execution on CIS-locale systems.
Process self-DACL set to deny-Everyone via KZsyzkgZlDQw.UB, preventing other processes from inspecting or terminating the ransomware process.
Memory-pig killer heuristic: processes consuming more than 250 MB are terminated, targeting sandbox environments and active debuggers.
ARP table parsed via arp -a; ARP-driven Wake-On-LAN packets sent to discovered hosts to bring them online before encryption.
Network shares enumerated via net view, NetDfsEnum, WNetEnumResource*, Win32_Share WMI class, and Win32_NetworkConnection.
Anti-analysis kill list matched against ProcessName and MainWindowTitle to identify and terminate security/analysis tools.

techniques.technique_name: System Location Discovery: System Language Discovery
techniques.technique_details: CIS culture/LCID check used as exclusion geo-fence; execution aborts if system locale matches CIS-region languages.
net.exe use \\<unc> executed over discovered SMB shares to access and encrypt remote file systems.
WebClient.DownloadData referenced in Helpers.DownloadUrl — present in code but not reachable at runtime (inert code path).
AES-128-CBC PKCS#7 / NoPadding (large files) encryption of all eligible files on local drives, mounted unlettered volumes, and LAN shares.
ServiceController.Stop called against ~100 services (AV, backup, MSSQL, Exchange, MBAM, Veeam, Acronis, etc.) combined with sc config <svc> start=disabled to prevent restart.
VSS deletion via PowerShell or vssadmin, SRRemoveRestorePoint, backup file deletion (del /s /q), Recycle Bin removal (rd /s /q), Restart Manager used to force-shutdown apps locking target files.

medusa.description: The medusa ransomware group is a highly sophisticated cybercriminal syndicate known for target-rich double extortion campaigns. Sourcing initial access widely, often through infostealer credential sales on underground forums.

worldleaks.description: World Leaks emerged in January 2025 as a rebrand of the Hunters International ransomware operation, shifting its focus from file encryption to solely stealing sensitive data and threatening to leak it unless a ransom is paid

killsec.description: KillSec originated as a hacktivist group aligned with the Anonymous movement before pivoting to ransomware operations in October 2023, officially launching a RaaS platform in June 2024 with an affiliate-friendly 88% revenue split, primarily targeting healthcare, financial services, and government sectors with over 250 documented victims as of late 2025.

nova.description: Nova (formerly RALord) is a ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) group that encrypts victims’files and uses double-extortion tactics to pressure organizations into paying for decryption and data non-disclosure.

rhysida.description: The rhysida ransomware group is a highly sophisticated cybercriminal syndicate known for target-rich double extortion campaigns. Sourcing initial access widely, often through infostealer credential sales on underground forums.

shinyhunters.description: ShinyHunters is a financially motivated data-theft and extortion group active since 2020, responsible for high-profile breaches including Ticketmaster (via Snowflake) and PowerSchool; by 2025 they launched a RaaS offering called "shinysp1d3r," and in August 2025 French authorities arrested four members.

techniques.technique_details: Scanning GitHub/Bitbucket repositories for exposed API keys and OAuth tokens (Secret Hunting).
Voice calls to helpdesk pretending to be employees to obtain password reset or MFA bypass.

techniques.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
techniques.technique_details: Use of malicious "Connected" OAuth applications to maintain persistent access to Salesforce and Microsoft 365 environments without needing passwords.
Frequent targets include Confluence, Jira, and SharePoint to collect internal documentation and plaintext credentials.
Use of legitimate cloud tools and native APIs to "drain" data from CRMs (Salesforce) directly to C2 servers.
